theorem.class9.product_log.title: Product Law of Logarithms
theorem.class10.alternate_segment.title: Theorem 10.6: Alternate Segment Theorem
theorem.class11.triangle_midpoint.title: Triangle Midpoint Theorem
theorem.class11.trapezium_midpoint.title: Trapezium Midpoint Theorem
theorem.class11.trapezium_midpoint.step3: 🧮 M₁M₂ = ½[(b⃗ − ã) + (c⃗ − d⃗)] = ½(AB + DC). The sum of the parallel side vectors, halved.
theorem.class11.trapezium_midpoint.step4: ✅ Since AB ∥ DC, M₁M₂ is parallel to both. Its length = ½(|AB| + |DC|). For a bridge with top=10m and bottom=16m: midspan = ½(10+16) = 13m. This is why the trapezoidal rule in calculus uses the average of endpoints!
theorem.class11.trapezium_midpoint.pract: Trapezium with AB=8cm, DC=12cm (parallel). Find the length of the segment joining midpoints of non-parallel sides.
theorem.class11.geometric_sum.title: Sum of First n Terms of a Geometric Series
theorem.class11.geometric_sum.finalFormu: Sum of a geometric series: Sₙ = a₁(1−rⁿ)/(1−r) for r ≠ 1.
theorem.class11.geometric_sum.keyInsight: This formula explains why a ball bouncing to 60% of its height each time travels a finite total distance despite bouncing infinitely — the infinite sum converges! It's also how compound interest calculations work: your bank balance grows geometrically.
theorem.class11.arithmetico_geometric_su: 🧮 Subtracting: (1−r)Sₙ = a + dr + dr² + ... + drⁿ⁻¹ − (a+nd)rⁿ. The a terms telescope, and the d terms form a geometric series itself!
✅ The middle dr terms form a geometric series: dr(1+r+...+rⁿ⁻²) = dr(1−rⁿ⁻¹)/(1−r). Dividing through by (1−r) gives the complete formula. This is how actuaries calculate tiered annuity payments!
a=1, d=2, r=3, n=2. Find S₂ (first term = a=1, second term = (a+d)r = 9).
Write out the first n terms and add them
theorem.class11.remainder_theorem.title: Remainder Theorem
theorem.class11.remainder_theorem.finalF: The remainder when dividing by (x−c) equals p(c).
theorem.class11.remainder_theorem.keyIns: This theorem lets you evaluate polynomials at a point without substituting! It's the foundation of synthetic division and is used in computer graphics for fast polynomial evaluation (Horner's method).
theorem.class11.remainder_theorem.step1_: 📈 You're a data scientist fitting a polynomial to temperature data. You need to know the value at a specific time t = c. The Remainder Theorem lets you find it without computing the whole polynomial!
theorem.class11.remainder_theorem.step2_: 📐 Substitute x = c into the identity: p(c) = (c−c)q(c) + r. The (c−c) term becomes zero.
theorem.class11.remainder_theorem.step3_: 🧮 Since c−c = 0, the term (c−c)q(c) = 0 × q(c) = 0. Therefore p(c) = r. The remainder when dividing p(x) by (x−c) equals p(c) — the polynomial evaluated at x = c!
theorem.class11.remainder_theorem.step4_: ✅ This theorem is the basis of synthetic division — a shortcut that divides polynomials using only coefficients. It's vastly faster for high-degree polynomials and is used in computer graphics for Bézier curve evaluation (Horner's method reduces O(n²) to O(n))!
theorem.class11.remainder_theorem.practi: p(x) = x² − 5x + 6. Find the remainder when divided by (x−2).
theorem.class11.factor_theorem.step3_det: 🔄 Conversely, if p(c) = 0, then by the Remainder Theorem the remainder r = 0. Substituting r = 0: p(x) = (x−c)q(x). This proves (x−c) is a factor.
theorem.class11.factor_theorem.step4_det: ✅ For p(x) = x³ − 2x² − x + 2: p(1) = 1 − 2 − 1 + 2 = 0. So (x−1) is a factor! Dividing: p(x) = (x−1)(x² − x − 2) = (x−1)(x−2)(x+1). One root found, the rest follows easily!
theorem.class11.factor_theorem.practice_: Check p(2). If p(2) = 0, it's a factor.
theorem.class11.combination_formula.fina: Number of ways to choose r objects from n distinct objects.
theorem.class11.combination_formula.keyI: Combinations count selections without regard to order. If you're picking a 3-person committee from 10 people, the order you pick them doesn't matter — that's ¹⁰C₃ = 120 ways! This formula is the foundation of probability calculations.
theorem.class11.combination_formula.prac: ⁵C₂ = 10. There are 10 ways to choose 2 from 5.
theorem.class11.binomial_theorem.title: Binomial Theorem for Positive Integers
theorem.class11.binomial_theorem.finalFo: Expand (a+b)ⁿ as a sum of terms with binomial coefficients.
theorem.class11.binomial_theorem.keyInsi: The binomial theorem is the algebraic powerhouse behind probability (binomial distribution), finance (binomial options pricing model), and even fractal geometry (Pascal's Triangle generates the Sierpinski triangle!).
theorem.class11.binomial_theorem.step1_d: 🌳 You're a geneticist tracking how a trait spreads through a family tree. Each generation inherits one allele from each parent. The expansion of (A+B)ⁿ gives all possible genotype combinations!
theorem.class11.fundamental_trig.title: Fundamental Law of Trigonometry: cos(α−β)
theorem.class11.fundamental_trig.step1_d: 🎯 You're a GPS engineer. The unit circle represents the Earth's equator, and points C and D are two satellites at angles α and β from the prime meridian.
theorem.class11.fundamental_trig.step2_d: 📐 The central angle between C and D is α−β. Construct angle AOB = α−β with A(1,0) and B(cos(α−β), sin(α−β)). Since the central angles are equal, chords CD and AB are equal.
theorem.class11.fundamental_trig.step3_d: 🧮 Equating CD² = AB² and expanding both sides. On the left: cos²α + cos²β − 2cosαcosβ + sin²α + sin²β − 2sinαsinβ = 2 − 2(cosαcosβ + sinαsinβ). On the right: 1 − 2cos(α−β) + 1 = 2 − 2cos(α−β).
theorem.class11.fundamental_trig.step4_d: ✅ Equating: 2 − 2(cosαcosβ + sinαsinβ) = 2 − 2cos(α−β). Cancel 2 and divide by −2: cos(α−β) = cosαcosβ + sinαsinβ. From this single identity flows ALL of trigonometry!
theorem.class11.fundamental_trig.practic: cos(60°−30°) = cos 60° cos 30° + sin 60° sin 30°. Compute cos(30°).
theorem.class11.trig_identities.title: Trigonometric Identities (Double & Triple Angle)
theorem.class11.trig_identities.finalFor: Double-angle formulas derived from the fundamental law.
theorem.class11.trig_identities.keyInsig: Double and triple angle formulas are essential in physics (harmonic oscillators, wave interference), engineering (Fourier series decomposing signals into frequencies), and even music synthesis (generating overtones from base frequencies)
theorem.class11.trig_identities.step1_de: 🧭 Navigation: If you turn 30° clockwise vs 30° counterclockwise, the eastward component is the same! By the fundamental law with α=0: cos(−β) = cos 0 cos β + sin 0 sin β = cos β.
theorem.class12.power_rule.title: Power Rule for Differentiation
theorem.class12.power_rule.step1_detail: 📈 A rocket scientist needs to calculate the velocity of a spacecraft at every instant. Position s(t) = t³. By definition, velocity = derivative = limit of (s(t+h)−s(t))/h as h→0.
theorem.class12.power_rule.step2_detail: 📐 Expand (x+h)ⁿ using the binomial theorem. The terms are: xⁿ + n·xⁿ⁻¹·h + n(n−1)/2!·xⁿ⁻²·h² + ... + hⁿ.
theorem.class12.power_rule.step3_detail: 🧮 Subtract xⁿ and divide by h. The xⁿ cancels, and every remaining term contains h. Factor h: the numerator becomes h[n·xⁿ⁻¹ + ... + hⁿ⁻¹]. Cancel h with denominator.
theorem.class12.power_rule.step4_detail: ✅ As h→0, all terms with h vanish, leaving only n·xⁿ⁻¹. For the rocket: if s(t) = t³, velocity v(t) = 3t². At t=5 seconds: v = 3×25 = 75 m/s. The power rule makes differentiation instant!
theorem.class12.power_rule.practice_hint: d/dx xⁿ = n·xⁿ⁻¹, so d/dx x⁵ = 5·x⁴
theorem.class12.sin_derivative.title: Derivative of Sine Function
theorem.class12.sin_derivative.finalForm: The derivative of sin x is cos x.
theorem.class12.sin_derivative.keyInsigh: This is the foundation of all trigonometric calculus! The derivative of sine is cosine because the slope of sin x at any point equals the value of cos x at that point. This relationship is what makes simple harmonic motion (springs, pendulums) so elegant: d²x/dt² = −ω²x.
theorem.class12.sin_derivative.step1_det: 🎵 An audio engineer analyzing sound waves needs to know how the amplitude changes. A sine wave sin(440t) represents musical note A440. The rate of change gives the velocity of the speaker cone.
theorem.class12.sin_derivative.step2_det: 📐 Use the identity sin(A+B) = sin A cos B + cos A sin B: sin(x+h) = sin x cos h + cos x sin h.
theorem.class12.sin_derivative.step3_det: 🧮 Factor: = lim[sin x(cos h − 1)/h + cos x(sin h)/h]. Two special limits emerge!
theorem.class12.sin_derivative.step4_det: ✅ The two fundamental limits: (1) lim(cos h − 1)/h = 0 (the graph of cos is flat at h=0), (2) lim(sin h)/h = 1 (the small-angle approximation). Result: d/dx sin x = sin x·0 + cos x·1 = cos x. Similarly, d/dx cos x = −sin x.
theorem.class12.inverse_trig_derivatives: Derivatives of inverse trig functions using implicit differentiation.

Inverse trig derivatives appear in many integration problems (∫ 1/√(1−x²) dx = sin⁻¹ x + C). They're also essential in physics for calculating angles from ratios, like finding the launch angle of a projectile from its velocity components!
Let y = sin⁻¹ x ⇒ x = sin y

🎯 A ballistics expert measures the ratio of vertical to horizontal velocity (x) and needs the angle. If x = 0.5, then y = sin⁻¹(0.5) = 30°. Implicit differentiation finds how the angle changes with the ratio.
📐 Using implicit differentiation: d/dx(x) = d/dx(sin y) → 1 = cos y · dy/dx → dy/dx = 1/cos y.
🧮 Using the identity cos²y + sin²y = 1: cos y = √(1−sin²y) = √(1−x²). Therefore d/dx sin⁻¹ x = 1/√(1−x²).
✅ For tan⁻¹: differentiate x = tan y → 1 = sec²y·dy/dx. Since sec²y = 1 + tan²y = 1 + x², we get d/dx tan⁻¹ x = 1/(1+x²). These formulas are the key to integrating rational functions!
theorem.class12.basic_integrals.title: Basic Indefinite Integrals
theorem.class12.basic_integrals.finalFor: Basic integral formulas derived from reverse differentiation.
theorem.class12.basic_integrals.keyInsig: Integration is the inverse of differentiation. Every differentiation rule gives an integration rule! The power rule d/dx xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) = xⁿ reverses to ∫ xⁿ dx = xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) + C. This is the foundation of all integral calculus.
theorem.class12.basic_integrals.step1_de: 🏗️ A civil engineer calculating the volume of an irregular foundation uses integration. If the cross-sectional area is A(x) = x², the volume from x=0 to x=3 is ∫₀³ x² dx = [x³/3]₀³ = 9 cubic meters.
theorem.class12.basic_integrals.step2_de: 📐 The power rule fails when n = −1 (would give division by zero). But d/dx[ln x] = 1/x, so ∫ 1/x dx = ln|x| + C. This is the bridge between algebraic and logarithmic functions.
theorem.class12.basic_integrals.step3_de: 🧮 Differentiate aˣ/ln a: the derivative is aˣ·ln a / ln a = aˣ. Therefore ∫ aˣ dx = aˣ/ln a + C. For the natural exponential: ∫ eˣ dx = eˣ + C (since ln e = 1).
theorem.class12.basic_integrals.step4_de: ✅ Integration is linear: the integral of a sum is the sum of integrals, and constants factor out. This is why ∫ (3x² + 2x + 1) dx = 3·x³/3 + 2·x²/2 + x + C = x³ + x² + x + C.
theorem.class12.basic_integrals.practice: ∫ x³ dx = ? (Evaluate at x=2 minus x=0)
theorem.class12.substitution_integrals.f: Standard integrals derived via trigonometric substitution.
theorem.class12.substitution_integrals.k: Trigonometric substitution transforms square roots into trigonometric functions! √(a²−x²) → x = a sin θ → a cos θ, eliminating the radical. This technique was used by Newton to derive the area of a circle: ∫ √(a²−x²) dx gives the area of a circular segment!
